name: Ethics of Risk
title: EF-04 — Ethics of Risk (Multi-Principle Hybrid)
foundation: No single risk-distribution principle is universally adequate. A defensible AV ethical framework must combine three complementary principles: Bayesian aggregate harm minimization, Equality of risk across individuals, and Maximin protection of the worst-off party. These are weighted by configurable coefficients that can be adjusted per jurisdiction or deployment context. A hard maximum acceptable risk threshold R_max ensures no individual bears intolerable risk regardless of how weights are set.
decision_logic: Total cost = w_B * C_Bayes + w_E * C_Equality + w_M * C_Maximin + responsibility_adjustment. C_Bayes = sum of Risk_i (aggregate harm). C_Equality = variance of Risk_i across road users (distributional fairness). C_Maximin = max_i(Risk_i) (worst-case protection). Responsibility adjustment reduces the cost assigned to trajectories where a non-AV road user is behaving illegally or dangerously, preventing the AV from being penalised for others' violations. Hard constraint: Risk_i must not exceed R_max for any individual i. Select trajectory minimising total cost subject to this constraint. Weights w_B, w_E, w_M sum to 1 and are configurable per jurisdiction.
pros: Most comprehensive ethical formalisation — integrates three complementary distributional principles
Responsibility adjustment prevents penalising the AV for dangerous behaviour by other road users
Configurable weights allow cultural and jurisdictional adaptation without changing the algorithm
EU ethical guidelines aligned — empirically validated across 2,000+ scenarios
Subsumes EF-01, equality, and EF-03 as special cases when weights are set to extremes
R_max threshold provides a safety floor ensuring no single individual bears intolerable risk
cons: Weight selection is unresolved — no consensus on the correct values for w_B, w_E, w_M
Complexity increases implementation burden and reduces interpretability for non-experts
Responsibility modeling is incomplete in practice — assigning blame across parties is hard
Harm quantification via delta_v to injury probability introduces uncertainty in C_Bayes
Three-component cost function makes it harder to explain a single decision in simple terms
best_fit_scenarios: complex urban intersections with mixed road user types and vulnerability levels
scenarios requiring balanced treatment of aggregate welfare and individual rights
policy-level deployment where explicit ethical parameter tuning is institutionally justified
mixed stakeholders with both VRUs and vehicle occupants present
jurisdictions requiring explicit documentation of the ethical framework used
poor_fit_scenarios: real-time embedded systems with strict computational latency constraints
scenarios where a single ethical principle clearly dominates making the hybrid unnecessary
contexts where weight transparency creates political or legal sensitivity
simple routine driving scenarios where EF-02 alone is sufficient
use_when: always — EF-04 is the shared analytical substrate used to set weights (bayesian, equality, maximin) regardless of which framework is dominant
in contributing_frameworks whenever the three-component cost breakdown informed the reasoning
to explain how aggregate harm, distributional fairness, and worst-case protection were balanced in the final decision
avoid_when: as dominant_framework — EF-04 is never dominant; it is the mathematical substrate within which EF-01, EF-02, EF-03, and EF-05 operate
dominant_when: never — EF-04 cannot be dominant_framework; set it only in contributing_frameworks
tradeoffs: vs Utilitarian (EF-01): EF-04 with w_B=1, w_E=0, w_M=0 equals EF-01 — but in practice w_E and w_M add fairness and protection terms that EF-01 ignores entirely. vs Maximin (EF-03): EF-04 with w_M=1, w_B=0, w_E=0 equals EF-03 — but hybrid weighting prevents pathological sacrifice of many for one. vs Deontological (EF-02): EF-02 defines the feasible set; EF-04 selects the best trajectory within it — they are complementary not competing. vs EVT (EF-05): EF-04 uses objective risk magnitudes; EVT adds subjective social valence weighting on top of harm — they address different dimensions.
key_parameters: w_B weight for Bayesian aggregate component (default 0.4)
R_max maximum acceptable risk per individual
responsibility_factor r_i per road user based on compliance with traffic rules
source_papers: Geisslinger et al. (2021) — Autonomous Driving Ethics: from Trolley Problem to Ethics of Risk
Geisslinger et al. (2023) — An Ethical Trajectory Planning Algorithm for Autonomous Vehicles
